coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-induced carbon-coated Ni and Mo2N nanosheets for efficient hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Fan
Tang, Yang
Zhao, Jiaming
Bai, Yu
Chen, Jiucun
Tian, Liangliang
Shah, Syed Shoaib Ahmad
Bao, Shu-juan
coredata.dc:description: The insufficient intrinsic activity and electrical conductivity of transition metal-based catalysts still inhibits their practical application. In this work, carbon-coated nano-Ni-armed Mo2N nanosheets (Ni@C-Mo2N) assembled on Ni Foam (NF) by using CDs-NH2 as reaction inducing and welding reagent. In-depth experiments reveal that the functional groups of (CDs) play a decisive role in regulating the phase composition and hierarchical structure of the final hybrid material. As electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), the optimized hierarchical Ni@C-Mo2N/NF achieved a very low overpotential of 38 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding stability of over 60 h, which should from the synergistic interfacial effect among the Ni, Mo2N and graphitic carbon optimized the electronic structure of catalyst with enhanced active sites, and improved electrical conductivity. This work provides novel insights for constructing more hierarchical multicomponent materials with the help of variable functional groups of CDs for further electrochemical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Functional group
Molybdenum nitride
Nickel nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis and microstructure of the Ni@C-Mo2N electrocatalyst; (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs with different function groups; (c) XRD pattern of NiMoO4, Ni/MoO2-C and Ni@C-Mo2N; (d) Raman spectrum of NiMoO4, Ni/MoO2-C and Ni@C-Mo2N; (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs-NH2, precursor and Ni@C-Mo2N.
XPS spectra of the precursor and Ni@C-Mo2N/NF. (a) full spectrum of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF and its precursor and high-resolution spectra of (b) Ni 2p, (c) Mo 3d and (d) N 1s in Ni@C-Mo2N/NF.
(a, b) SEM images, (c) TEM and (d-f) HRTEM images of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF. (g) STEM images and the corresponding EDX elemental mapping images of C, N, Mo and Ni taken from Ni@C-Mo2N/NF.

(a) LSV curves and (b) Tafel plots of NF, Mo-Ni-O/NF, Mo-Ni-CDs-NH2/NF, NiMoO4/NF, Ni/MoO2-C/NF and Ni@C-Mo2N/NF; (c) Comparison of overpotential (η10) at the current density of 10 mA cm−2; (d) EIS Nyquist plots recorded on Mo-Ni-O/NF, Mo-Ni-CDs-NH2/NF, NiMoO4/NF, Ni/MoO2-C/NF and Ni@C-Mo2N/NF electrodes with the potential set to 0.1 V vs RHE; (e) 60 h chronoamperometry (at a static overpotential of 45 mV) of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF; (f) LSV curves of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF before and after the i-t stability test in 1M KOH (The data is presented without IR-correction).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-induced carbon-coated Ni and Mo2N nanosheets for efficient hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University Key Laboratory of Micro/Nano Materials Engineering and Technology
Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bChongqing University Key Laboratory of Micro/Nano Materials Engineering and Technology, Chongqing University of Arts and Sciences, Yongchuan, Chongqing 402160, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The insufficient intrinsic activity and electrical conductivity of transition metal-based catalysts still inhibits their practical application. In this work, carbon-coated nano-Ni-armed Mo2N nanosheets (Ni@C-Mo2N) assembled on Ni Foam (NF) by using CDs-NH2 as reaction inducing and welding reagent. In-depth experiments reveal that the functional groups of (CDs) play a decisive role in regulating the phase composition and hierarchical structure of the final hybrid material. As electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), the optimized hierarchical Ni@C-Mo2N/NF achieved a very low overpotential of 38 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding stability of over 60 h, which should from the synergistic interfacial effect among the Ni, Mo2N and graphitic carbon optimized the electronic structure of catalyst with enhanced active sites, and improved electrical conductivity. This work provides novel insights for constructing more hierarchical multicomponent materials with the help of variable functional groups of CDs for further electrochemical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical water splitting to produce hydrogen is one of the most promising alternative technologies for the development of clean, pollution-free, and sustainable chemical energy sources [1–4]. However, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is thermodynamically sluggish and requires a high overpotential to provide a suitable and spontaneous reaction rate [5–7]. In order to reduce energy consumption and improve the efficiency of hydrogen production, the development of highly efficient HER electrocatalysts is of great significance [8,9]. To date, among the various HER catalysts, platinum (Pt) is one of the most effective catalysts [10–12]. Nevertheless, the scarcity and high cost of Pt-based electrocatalysts hinder the large-scale commercial applications [13,14]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop an inexpensive, efficient, and stable alternatives for commercial viablility of this promising technology [5,15,16]. Several transition-metal based electrocatalysts have been developed with efficient performances [17–19], however, their performance is still inferior to commercial requirements.

Transition-metals nitride [20–22], also known as interstitial alloys, have received extensive attention for electrocatalytic water splitting because of their hydrogenase-like catalytic mechanism, excellent corrosion resistance, high conductivity, and stability. Especially, molybdenum nitrides (Mo2N) [23–25] as an important member of transition-metals nitride, possess Pt-like metal-structures, which negatively-charged nitrogen by modifying the d-band density of Mo. However, Mo2N only has relatively moderate activity for HER, owing to strong metal hydride (MH) bond of Mo [26,27]. Combining a MH-strong metal (Mo) with MH-weak metal may be an effective method to optimize electronic structure of catalysts and H* adsorption on their surface. Moreover, it has been reported that Mo-based catalysts modified by a MH-weak metal (Ni) can greatly improve HER activity of the composite. For instance, Yong Wang's group [28] greatly improved the catalytic performance of MoxC-Ni@NCV via the synergistic effect between γ-MoC and Ni. Liqiang Mai group [29] achieved enhanced electrocatalytic HER activity and excellent stability by the incorporation of Ni into MoO2. Hence, we anticipated that designing a hierarchical multicomponent composite of Mo2N and Ni is promising for constructing an excellent HER electrocatalyst. In addition to the composition, the exposure of active sites significantly affects the catalytic performance. Yet, constructing an efficient heterogeneous Mo/Ni catalytic interface remains extremely challenging.

It is generally believed that coupling the catalyst with carbon material can effectively increase the charge transfer rate and minimize nanoparticle aggregation in the multicomponent catalyst, thereby enhancing catalytic activity and stability [30]. Among these carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely considered as nano-level advanced materials for improving catalytic performance of various composites due to their tiny size, high electric conductivity, low cost, and environmentally-friendly nature [31–35]. More importantly, due to their abundant and adjustable functional groups, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit a favorable coupling ability, which can provide high-density binding sites to immobilize and confine the growth and aggregation of compound catalysts, for further tailoring and regulating electrocatalytic reaction sites. Therefore, combining CDs with other materials as multifunctional precursors for engineering multicomponent catalysts have been studied [36–38]. However, phase composition of multicomponent catalysts induced by CDs is rarely studied.

In this paper, we propose a powerful strategy for constructing three-dimensional hierarchical multicomponent catalysts by pyrolytic reduction of Mo-Ni/carbon dots (CDs) precursor. After pyrolysis under reducing atmosphere, carbon-coated nano-Ni-armed Mo2N nanosheets (Ni@C-Mo2N) were prepared. The Ni@C-Mo2N hybrid electrocatalyst exhibited a very low overpotential (38 mV) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding stability of over 60 h in alkaline solution for HER. More surprising, when catalyst induced by CD-COOH instead of CDs-NH2, a different phase composition was obtained (Ni/MoO2-C) and also exhibited great catalytic performance for HER. The combination strategy induced by CDs offers a simple and effective method to create highly-efficient hierarchical multicomponent catalysts.

NiCl2•6H2O, Na2MoO4 and KOH were obtained from Aladdin Co. Ltd, China. All the reagents were used directly without any further purification. Deionized water (DI) with a specific resistance of 18.25 MΩ cm−1 was prepared in a purification instrument (Millipore Elix® Advantage 10, MERCK MILLIPORE).

For the follow-up research, we prepared two kinds of carbon dots with different functional groups. First, 10 g citric acid (CA) and 1 g basic fuchsin were ground to form a homogeneous mixture and added to a 100-mL beaker. Afterwards, the beaker was heated to 300°C for 5 min. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the CDs-NH2 were obtained. CDs-COOH were obtained under the same conditions, except without adding basic fuchsin.

A piece of NF (2 cm × 3 cm) was cleaned by ethanol, concentrated HCl solution, and deionized water with ultrasonication for 10 min, respectively, and subsequently dried in vacuum at 60°C overnight. The precursor was synthesized by a microwave hydrothermal process. Briefly, the prepared solution containing 50 mL deionized water, 0.5 mmol NiCl2•6H2O, 0.5 mmol Na2MoO4•2H2O, and 186 mg CDs-NH2 was transferred into a 100-mL microwave reaction vessel, placed in a microwave synthesis system, then heated to 160°C at 500 W for 30 min. After the reaction, the precursor was washed with deionized water several times, dried at 60°C for 12 h, and denoted as Mo-Ni-CDs-NH2/NF. Then, Ni@C-Mo2N/NF was obtained by calcination of the precursor in a tube furnace at 500°C for 2 h in 5% H2/Ar. In order to investigate the effect of calcination temperature on the microstructure and performance of final products, the precursor was also calcined at different temperatures. For comparison, NiMoO4/NF was obtained under the same conditions but without adding CDs. To compare the effects of CDs with different functional groups on the microstructure and performance of final products, Ni/MoO2-C/NF was also obtained by replacing CDs-NH2 with CDs-COOH under the same synthesis conditions. In this work, the average mass loading of Ni foam was about 1.8 mg cm−2.

Phase compositions of the catalysts were determined by powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-7000). Morphology of the catalysts were observed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7800F, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 and FEI TF20). The Raman spectroscopy reflected the disorder and defect of catalyst was performed on Raman Spectrometer (LabRAM HR, JY Evolution, 532 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250xi, USA) was used to analyze the surface compositions and element chemical state of the catalysts. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Tensor 207) was used to analyze the functional groups.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, Chenhua Instruments Inc.) in 1 M KOH, Hg/HgO electrode (1 M KOH) and carbon rod were used as the reference and counter electrode. Linear scan voltammograms (LSV) were measured at 5 mV s−1 in a three-electrode system. The long-term stability test was performed by i-t chronoamperometry. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were detected with a frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The main arc in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra was matched utilizing a simplified Randles equivalent circuit, which was composed of a resistance (Rs) in series with a parallel arrangement of a charge-transfer resistance (Rct) and a constant phase element (CPE), and the fitting parameters were appraised through the Levenberg-Marquardt minimization procedure. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was applied to measure the electrochemical double layer capacitance at nonfaradaic potentials as another way to reckon the efficient electrochemical active area of HER. The overpotential values used in this work were calculated according to the following equation: E (overpotential, V) = E (vs. Hg/HgO) + 0.098 + 0.0592pH.

The CDs-assisted self-assembly method is utilized for growing carbon- coated nano-Ni armed Mo2N sheets (Ni@C-Mo2N) on nickel foam (NF) as presented in Fig. 1a. The two kinds of CDs with different function groups but similar morphologies (Fig. S1) were used to fabricate the Ni-Mo based composites. The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 1b) confirmed the two CDs: one with amino groups (named CDs-NH2) and the other with carboxyl groups (named CDs-COOH) [32,39,40]. After a microwave hydrothermal treatment, the XRD of the precursors with or without CDs were basically same (Fig. S2), while completely different XRD patterns of the final samples were obtained after annealing at 500°C in H2/Ar atmosphere (Fig. 1c). The XRD pattern of the sample obtained without adding carbon dots is very similar to that of NiMoO4 (JCPDF no. 45-0142) [41], while the characteristic diffraction peaks of the sample formed by adding CDs-COOH belong to MoO2 (JCPDF no. 32-0671) and metal Ni (JCPDF no. 04-0850). Interestingly, the diffraction peaks at 37.4°, 43.5°, 63.1°, and 75.7° of sample obtained by adding CDs-NH2 can be respectively assigned to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of the Mo2N (JCPDF no. 25-1366) phase, and additional diffraction peaks can be indexed to Ni (JCPDF no. 04-0850). The aforementioned XRD results indicate that the functional groups of carbon dots can affect the phase composition of the catalyst. Besides, an obviously broad peak at around 22° was observed in the XRD spectrum of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF confirming the presence of graphite. The corresponding D and G bands at 1369 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d) further attest the formation of partially graphitic carbon [42]. It is worth noting that no typical carbon peak is observed in the XRD spectrum but is seen in the Raman spectrum of Ni/MoO2-C/NF (Fig. 1d). This is mainly because CDs-COOH with more oxygen functional groups are easier to reduce during the calcination process, resulting in low carbon content in the final composite. In order to further understand the evolution of CDs-NH2 during synthesis, FT-IR was carried out, and the results are displayed in Fig. 1ee. Compared with the characteristic peaks of CDs-NH2, the peak position of the amino functional group had a significant shift in the precursor, which suggests that CDs-NH2 was well-coupled with Ni2+ by electrostatic Columbic force. In the process of pyrolysis, nickel was first reduced to metal nanoparticles in a reducing atmosphere, then nano-Ni catalyzed CDs converted into graphitic carbon and coated on its surface [43]. At the same time, decomposed N from CDs binds to Mo, and form Mo2N, which indicating that CDs-NH2 play an important role in the formation of Ni@C-Mo2N.

The composition and surface chemical state of different elements in Ni@C-Mo2N/NF were further investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectra manifests the co-existence of Ni, Mo, C, N, and O elements in the precursor and Ni@C-Mo2N/NF (Fig. 2a). For Ni 2p spectrum of the precursor, two main peaks at 855.8 and 873.3 eV are corresponding to Ni2+. Compared with precursor, two new peaks (851.9 eV and 869.1 eV) of metallic state Ni0 are observed in the Ni 2p spectra of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF (Fig. 2b) [44], which is consistent with the XRD results. The presence of the Ni oxidation state on the surface of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF was inevitable due to exposure to air. In Fig. 2c, the Mo 3d spectra of precursor was split into two peaks at 232 and 235 eV refers to for Mo6+, Compared with precursor, the Mo 3d spectra of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF could fit six peaks, and the peaks appear at lower binging energies indicated that the Mo in the precursor is reduced to the lower valence state. Two peaks at 229.9 and 233.1 eV are corresponding to Mo4+, and peaks at 231.9 and 235.1 eV are attributed to Mo6+ [27,45]. Besides, the other peaks at 228.9 and 230.7 eV are defined as the binding energy of the Mo–N bond in Mo2N [26,46]. Formation of Mo-N bond is beneficial to increase corrosion resistance and conductivity of catalyst. The N 1s XPS spectrum for the precursor (Fig. 2d) shows the presence of the N 1s (398.1 eV), which is ascribed to the pyridinic N. The fine-scanned N 1s spectra of the Ni@C-Mo2N/NF catalyst fits two peaks. The peak at 398.1eV is same to the precursor, suggesting that carbon formed was doped by N [47,48]. The second peak at 395.6 eV corresponds to the formation of Mo-N, as reported in literature [25,27,49], which suggests the formation of new active sites.

The microstructure and morphology of the samples were further investigated by Field Emission Scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The SEM images of three precursors in Figs. S3–S5 indicate that Mo-Ni-CDs-NH2 presented different morphologies according to Mo-Ni-O and Mo-Ni-CDs-COOH, which implies that the functional groups of CDs can affect the microstructure of the products. After annealing, the morphology of the obtained samples showed little change with the different precursors (Figs. S6 and S7a, b). In Fig. 3 a, b, it is clear that the interconnected nanosheets with loose structures and abundant open spaces covered the surface of the nickel foam skeleton. More details of the samples were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As exhibited in Fig. 3c, some small nanoparticles can be seen in the nanosheets. For HR-TEM of the slack nanosheets in Fig. 3d, the lattice with a spacing of 0.208 nm is clearly observed, which matches well with the (200) facets of the Mo2N phase and reveals that the interconnected nanosheets consist of Mo2N nanocrystals. The enhanced images in Fig. 3e and f of dark particles from Fig. 3c reveal that these nanoparticles are approximately 5-10 nm, and the clear lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.203 nm correspond to the (111) facets of Ni (JCPDF no. 04-0850). It is also interesting to note these nano-Ni particles were covered by several carbon layers (Fig. 3f), whereby their lattice spacing is close to the spacing of (002) planes of graphitic carbon. Moreover, high angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) coupled with EDS element mapping (Fig. 3g) further reveal the homogenous distribution of Mo, N, and C on the nanosheets. While some small bright spots dispersed in the EDS of Ni. In addition, TEM images of the sample with added CDs-COOH (Ni/MoO2-C) in Fig. S7 reveals that only a small amount of carbon layer existed around nickel, which is agreement with XRD and Raman results.

The HER performance of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF was subsequently investigated in a typical three electrode electrochemical configuration with 1 M KOH electrolyte. First, the effect of annealing temperature on the catalytic performance was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the sample obtained at 500°C delivered better HER activity and lower charge transfer resistance than that samples obtained at higher temperatures, which may due to the slack nanosheet structure of the catalyst that was destroyed by calcinating in higher temperature (Figs. 2a, b and S8).

In contrast, the performance of NF, Mo-Ni-O/NF, Mo-Ni-CDs-NH2/NF, NiMoO4/NF, and Ni/MoO2-C/NF were tested under the same conditions. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves without iR correction are shown in Fig. 4a. It is obvious that the Ni@C-Mo2N/NF electrode exhibited superior HER performance with the lowest overpotential of only 38 mV at 10 mA cm−2 current density. Comparatively, NiMoO4/NF synthesized in the absence of CDs had a higher overpotential of 89 mV at 10 mA cm−2, demonstrating that the introduction of CDs could effectively enhance the HER activity of the corresponding products. The Tafel slopes of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF and Ni/MoO2-C/NF were calculated as 63.3 and 68.3 mV dec−1, respectively, implying that the HER processes followed a Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism and were lower than those of NF, Mo-Ni/NF, Mo-Ni-CDs/NF, and NiMoO4/NF (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the η10 value of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF surpasses most other related state-of-the-art electrocatalyst (Fig. 4c and Table S1,). To further explain the excellent performance of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF, the HER kinetics were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 4d, Ni@C-Mo2N/NF revealed the smallest semicircle in the Nyquist plot, demonstrating a lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) during the HER process and superior ion diffusion capability. The Rs value of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF was 2.01 Ω, which indicates great conductivity.

The electrochemical active surface area (ESCA) was measured by the electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) to estimate the active sites of the electrocatalyst. The Cdl value of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF was 107.8 mF cm−2 higher than those of NiMoO4/NF and Ni/MoO2-C/NF, which were calculated by measuring the capacitive current density at different scan rates (Fig. S10). These results are consistent with the polarization curves (Fig. 4a).

The long-term stability of the Ni@C-Mo2N/NF was evaluated at a constant applied voltage of -45 mV. Fig. 4e shows that the i-t curve of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF remained quite stable during continuous voltage output for 60 h. Moreover, after i-t testing, the material still exhibited great electrocatalytic activity with negligible attenuation, which indicates its excellent catalytic stability for HER (Fig. 4f). In addition, the morphology of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF remained almost unchanged after the i-t process, whereby the D and G peaks in the Raman spectrum were still obvious (Fig. S11). Moreover, the larger current density (50 mA cm−2) of the i-t testing curve and corresponding LSV curves confirm the outstanding stability of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF (Fig. S12), indicating the great potential of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF as high-performance and extremely stable HER electrocatalyst. Besides, the Ni/MoO2-C/NF catalyst displayed the good stability (Fig. S13), whereas the testing curve of NiMoO4/NF had a significant current loss (Fig. S14). In a word, both Ni/MoO2-C/NF and Ni@C-Mo2N/NF showed good stability because graphitic carbon could prevent the agglomeration of Ni and Mo2N nanosheets, thereby increasing structural stability.

The excellent catalytic activity of the unique Ni@C-Mo2N/NF can be attributed to the following aspects. (1) The unique hierarchical multicomponent structure creates abundant active sites for speeding up the HER kinetic process occurring on their interface by synergistic effects of multiple components; (2) The 3D skeleton and abundant open spaces of Ni@C-Mo2N/NF greatly increase the ECSA, providing a fast mass transfer for electrolytes; (3) The carbon-coated layer effectively inhibits Ni particles aggregation, enhances the stability of the catalyst, and increases exposure of the active centers; (4) Mo2N has strong H* adsorption and high conductivity, which can fast the electron transfer and improve the electrochemical utilization of active materials.

In this work, a novel carbon-coated nano-Ni armed Mo2N nanosheets hybrid catalyst (Ni@C-Mo2N) was assembled on Ni foam by an effective and simple CDs-induced combination strategy. The resulting hybrid catalyst with abundant well-dispersed active sites achieved rapid electron transfer and, as a HER electrocatalyst, exhibited excellent catalytic activity (38 mV@10 mA cm−2) and outstanding stability. Further control experiments revealed that the functional groups of CDs can effectively induce different phase composition and microstructure of the final hybrid material, which offers new opportunities to develop more highly-efficient hierarchical multicomponent catalysts for expanding their application potential.

Fan Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Yang Tang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Jiaming Zhao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yu Bai: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiucun Chen: Writing – review & editing. Liangliang Tian: Funding acquisition. Syed Shoaib Ahmad Shah: Writing – review & editing. Shu-juan Bao: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Chongqing University Key Laboratory of Micro/Nano Materials Engineering and Technology (KFJJ2002), Chongqing Doctoral Research and Innovation Project (CYB21106), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21972111, 21773188), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2018jcyjAX0714), and Venture & Innovation Support Program for Chongqing Overseas Returnees (cx2019073), Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices, and Chongqing Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials and Technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140671.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combining theory and experiment in electrocatalysis: insights into materials design
Science
